Catalyst/Technical-Papers/Concurrent-Ent.title: Concurrent Entity Creation in Catalyst
Catalyst/Technical-Papers/Concurrent-Ent.content:
Concurrent Entity Creation in Catalyst

Who this is for. This paper assumes you know what an Entity-Component-System (ECS) is — entities are IDs, components are data, and systems iterate over them. It does not assume you know how Catalyst stores that data internally, and it does not assume you know terms like chunk, spine, paged store, or stable addressing. Those are introduced from scratch. By the end you should understand not just what we built, but why each decision was made and what we would do differently.


1. Abstract
Catalyst is the Alchemy Engine’s ECS. Historically, like most ECS implementations, it required that all entity creation happen on a single thread — the “game thread.” This paper describes the work that removed that restriction. Any thread may now create an entity and immediately read or modify it, with correctness guaranteed by lock-free and lock-based primitives rather than by deferring the work to a safe point later.
The headline results:

Single spawns are callable from any thread and, when two threads spawn into the same archetype, they now run genuinely in parallel.
Bulk spawns (world load, procedural generation, asset registry population) run ~2.3× faster than the original single-threaded path.
Reads outside the frame tick (ForEach called from editor/UI code) are safe to run concurrently with spawns, and pay zero extra cost when no spawn is in flight.

Getting there required redesigning how Catalyst stores entity data so that memory never moves out from under a concurrent reader or writer. It also produced a few surprises — most notably that the thing we assumed was the bottleneck (a lock) was not, and the real cost was hiding somewhere far less obvious.

2. Goals and non-goals
The work was scoped tightly up front.
Goals

Any thread can create an entity and touch it immediately. Not “queue a request and get the entity next frame” — actually create it, get a handle back, and write to its components on the spot.
Bulk creation must be fast. Loading a world or generating terrain can create hundreds of thousands to millions of entities at once. That path must exploit all cores.
Prefabs and cross-library merges must work concurrently. These are the realistic ways entities get created in bulk, so they can’t be second-class.

Explicit non-goals

Concurrent destruction. Deleting entities from many threads at once is a much harder problem (it moves existing data around), and we decided it wasn’t worth the complexity yet. Where a system needs to “remove” an entity from multithreaded code, the intended pattern is entity disabling — flip a flag, and reap it later on the game thread. Concurrent destruction is discussed in Future work.

2.1 Why not just defer the work?
The obvious way to make entity creation “thread-safe” is to not actually do it on the calling thread. You record “please create this entity” into a per-thread command buffer, and a single thread plays all those commands back at a safe point. This is what an Entity Command Buffer (ECB) does, and Catalyst already has one.
We rejected ECB as the creation mechanism, deliberately. The whole point of the feature is “create it and touch it now.” If creation is deferred, you don’t have an entity yet — you have a promise of one — so you can’t read its components, can’t parent it to something, can’t hand its handle to another system this frame. Deferral solves a different problem (structural change during iteration) and would defeat the goal. So the rule became: creation is immediate; correctness comes from the data structures, not from postponing.

3. A primer on how Catalyst stores entities
To understand the concurrency problem, you first have to understand the storage. This section builds up the vocabulary. If you already know Catalyst’s internals, skim to §3.5.
3.1 Archetypes and columns
Catalyst groups entities by their exact set of components. Every unique set is an archetype. All entities that have exactly {Position, Velocity} live together in one archetype; entities with {Position, Velocity, Health} live in a different one.
Within an archetype, component data is stored column by column (a “structure of arrays” layout). One contiguous run of Position values, a separate run of Velocity values, and so on. Row N across all columns is entity N’s data. This layout is what makes iteration fast: a system that reads Position walks one tight, cache-friendly array.
graph TD
    subgraph Archetype["Archetype {Position, Velocity}"]
        P["Position column<br/>[P0][P1][P2][P3]..."]
        V["Velocity column<br/>[V0][V1][V2][V3]..."]
        H["EntityHandles<br/>[H0][H1][H2][H3]..."]
    end
    note["Row 2 = entity H2's Position P2 + Velocity V2"]

Each archetype also keeps a parallel EntityHandles array: row N holds the handle of the entity occupying that row. This is how iteration knows which entity it’s looking at.
3.2 Chunks
A column isn’t one giant allocation. It’s split into fixed-size blocks called chunks — in Catalyst, 16 KB each. A chunk holds as many rows as fit: for a 40-byte component, that’s ~400 rows per chunk. When a column fills its current chunks, it allocates another 16 KB chunk.
Chunks exist for cache and allocation reasons: 16 KB is a friendly size to allocate and to stream through the CPU cache, and you never have to copy a huge array to grow it — you just add a chunk.
3.3 The spine
If the data lives in chunks, something has to remember where the chunks are. That “list of pointers to chunks” is what we’ll call the spine. Think of it as the backbone of a column: the chunks are the ribs hanging off it.
spine:   [ ptr ][ ptr ][ ptr ]         <- an array of pointers
            |      |      |
            v      v      v
chunk 0 --16KB   chunk 1  chunk 2       <- the actual data

To find row N, you do two cheap operations: divide to pick the chunk (N / rows-per-chunk), and take the remainder to pick the slot inside it (N % rows-per-chunk). Because rows-per-chunk is a power of two, both are just bit shifts.
3.4 The entity handle
An EntityHandle is not a pointer. It’s a small value: an index plus a generation.

The index says which slot in a big table describes this entity — that table entry (the “record”) says which archetype the entity is in and which row.
The generation is a counter that lets us detect stale handles. When an entity is destroyed and its index is later reused by a different entity, the generation is bumped. An old handle still carries the old generation, so a liveness check (does the handle's generation still match the table's?) fails — the old handle correctly reports “dead,” even though its index now belongs to someone else.

This index→record table is the entity handle table. It’s central to everything that follows.
3.5 The one property that matters: stable addressing
Here is the concept the entire redesign hinges on.

Stable addressing means: once something has been given a memory address, that address never changes for the lifetime of the object — even as the container around it grows.

A plain growable array (std::vector) does not have this property. When it runs out of capacity, it allocates a bigger buffer, copies everything over, and frees the old buffer. Every pointer into the old buffer is now dangling. In single-threaded code that’s fine, because nobody is looking at the old buffer at the moment of the copy. In multithreaded code, it is a catastrophe — which is exactly the problem we hit.

4. Why naive concurrency breaks
Suppose we keep the storage as-is (growable arrays) and just let two threads spawn at once. What goes wrong?
Thread A is filling in a freshly created entity — writing its Position into a column. To do that, it reads the column’s spine to find the chunk, then writes into the chunk. Meanwhile Thread B spawns an entity too, the column needs to grow, and its spine (a growable array of chunk pointers) reallocates — the old spine buffer is freed. Thread A was mid-read of that exact buffer.
sequenceDiagram
    participant A as Thread A (filling row)
    participant S as Column spine (growable array)
    participant B as Thread B (growing column)
    A->>S: read spine to find my chunk
    B->>S: append chunk → REALLOCATE, free old spine
    A->>S: ...continues reading freed memory 💥

This is not theoretical. It crashed in testing with a segfault inside a memcpy, and it is the seed from which the whole design grew. The lesson: for concurrent creation to be safe, the storage that spawners and readers touch must never move.
Three separate things all need that guarantee:

The entity handle table — allocating an index must be safe from any thread, and a record, once addressed, must not move.
The archetype’s columns and EntityHandles — appending a row must not relocate existing rows or the pointers addressing them.
Everything hanging off a spawn — observer notifications, sparse components, slot hierarchies — must each be safe to touch concurrently.

The rest of the paper is those three problems and their solutions.

5. Building block: the lock-free entity handle table
The first structure we made concurrency-safe was the entity handle table. Its job: hand out unique entity indices to any thread, store each entity’s record (archetype + row), and never move a record once it exists.
The design mirrors a proven pattern already in the engine (the object manager), with one addition Catalyst needs.
5.1 A fixed spine that never reallocates
The trick to stable addressing is to make the spine fixed-size and inline. The table holds an array of exactly 1024 chunk pointers, allocated as part of the table itself — it is never resized, so it never moves. Each chunk holds 4096 entity slots. 1024 × 4096 ≈ 4.19 million entities per library.
Growth only ever means: install a chunk pointer into an empty spine slot. It never means “resize the spine.” Because the spine is fixed, a thread that already holds an entity index can compute that entity’s slot address and trust it forever.
Chunks[1024]   (fixed inline array — never moves)
   [0] --> Chunk (4096 slots)
   [1] --> Chunk (4096 slots)
   [2] --> nullptr   <- not allocated yet
   ...
   [1023] --> nullptr

To find a slot from an index: the top bits pick the chunk, the low 12 bits pick the slot within it. Two shifts and a load.
5.2 Compare-and-swap: growing without a lock
When a thread needs a chunk that doesn’t exist yet, how does it add one without a lock — and without two threads both allocating the same chunk?
The answer is an atomic compare-and-swap (CAS). A CAS says, atomically: “if this slot still holds the value I expect (nullptr), replace it with my new value; otherwise tell me it changed.” Two threads racing to install chunk 5 both allocate a candidate chunk and both attempt the CAS. Exactly one wins. The loser sees the CAS fail, throws away its candidate, and adopts the winner’s chunk. No lock, no duplicate, no lost update.
This “allocate speculatively, CAS, free-on-loss” idiom appears everywhere in this project. It is the standard way to grow a shared structure without a lock.
5.3 Recycling indices: the free-list
When an entity is destroyed, its index shouldn’t be wasted. It goes onto a free-list — a queue of indices available for reuse. New allocations prefer to pull from the free-list before extending into fresh territory.
The free-list is a moodycamel::ConcurrentQueue, a well-tested lock-free multi-producer/multi-consumer queue. Producers (threads freeing indices) and consumers (threads allocating) never block each other.
When an index is recycled, its generation is bumped — this is the mechanism from §3.4 that stales old handles. The generation lives in the slot as an atomic, and the bump is paired with the queue’s release/acquire ordering so that a thread which dequeues a recycled index is guaranteed to see the new generation.
5.4 The addition ObjectManager lacks: bulk reservation
A single Allocate() costs one atomic increment of the “next fresh index” counter. Fine for one entity. Terrible for a million: a million atomic increments, all contending on one counter.
So the table has AllocateRange(N): reserve N contiguous indices with one atomic fetch_add(N). A million-entity load pays a handful of atomics instead of a million. This is the path bulk spawning uses.
graph LR
    A["AllocateRange(1,000,000)"] --> B["one fetch_add(1,000,000)"]
    B --> C["indices [start .. start+1,000,000)"]

5.5 A small but real optimization: skip the lookup for fresh indices
A freshly reserved index has never been freed, so its generation is definitionally 0. We originally read the generation out of the slot for every handle we built — a needless two-step lookup through the chunk spine, a million times over during a load. Recognizing that fresh handles are always generation 0 let us skip the lookup entirely and stamp 0 directly. Small idea, measurable on the bulk path.

6. The heart of it: stable-address archetype storage
The handle table was the warm-up. The real work was making an archetype’s columns and EntityHandles stable-addressed, because that is what spawners write into and readers read from.
6.1 The problem with the fixed-spine approach here
You might ask: why not use the same fixed 1024-pointer spine we used for the handle table?
Because the handle table is one structure per library, but there are many columns — one per component type per archetype, across potentially hundreds of archetypes. A fixed spine large enough for a huge archetype (tens of thousands of chunk pointers) would be paid inline, per column, always, even for an archetype holding three entities. The memory blows up. We measured this tension and rejected the fixed spine for columns.
6.2 The solution: an inline-first, paged spine
Instead we made the spine paged, and put a small inline fast path in front of it. A column resolves a chunk’s address one of two ways:

Inline chunks. The first InlineChunkCount chunk pointers live directly inside the column, as a tiny inline array of atomics. A column that only ever holds a handful of chunks — the overwhelmingly common case, and every column of a narrow archetype — addresses its data straight out of these, and allocates no spine at all.
Overflow spine. Only when a column grows past its inline chunks does it lazily allocate the two-level spine: a fixed inline array of spine-block pointers, each spine-block a page of chunk-pointers, allocated on demand.

graph TD
    IC["InlineChunks[4]<br/>(inline atomics, ~32B — the only cost for a small column)"]
    IC -->|chunk 0| D0["16KB data chunk"]
    IC -->|chunk 1| D1["16KB data chunk"]
    IC -.->|grows past 4 chunks| SP["Overflow spine (lazy)"]
    SP --> SB["SpineBlocks[256]<br/>(allocated only on overflow)"]
    SB -->|block 0| B0["Block: 256 chunk-pointers"]
    B0 -->|ptr 0| C0["16KB data chunk"]

Both levels grow lazily by CAS, exactly like §5.2, and nothing ever moves once installed — not a data chunk, not a spine-block, not the inline array. That is the stable-addressing guarantee. The always-paid cost is now just the inline pointers (a few dozen bytes per column); everything heavier is allocated only when a column actually grows large enough to need it.

Why the inline fast path exists — a measured blow-up. A two-level spine on its own still carried a ~2KB inline top array per column, plus a ~2KB first spine-block the moment a column held any data. §6.1 warned that a fixed per-column spine makes memory blow up for narrow archetypes — and a couple of kilobytes, paid per column, does exactly that once you have many columns. A stress spec that adds 700 distinctly-named slots to a single entity walks it through archetypes of width 1, 2, … 700, creating ~245,000 columns; at ~4KB of pure addressing each, that summed to ~1GB. It was also slow, because building each wide archetype and reserving each row did O(width) spine and block allocations. Moving the common case inline collapsed it: the same spec dropped from ~1190MB to ~280MB and from ~650ms to ~200ms, with no change to the concurrency guarantees. It is the §6.1 lesson applied one level deeper — don’t pay for capacity a column doesn’t use.

Tuning InlineChunkCount. It defaults to 4: four inline chunk pointers, ~32 bytes paid on every column of every archetype, covering any column that stays within four chunks before it promotes to the overflow spine. Because that tax is universal, it is deliberately small. Raise it only if profiling shows a workload with many archetypes that each sit just above the inline count and are churning spine promotions — the trade is a few more always-paid bytes per column against fewer lazy spine allocations for those mid-sized archetypes. The opposite shape — a handful of archetypes each holding millions of entities — is indifferent to the value: those columns promote to the overflow spine almost immediately and amortize it over millions of rows, so leave it at the default. When unsure, keep it at 4; it is sized for the narrow-archetype case, which is where the memory actually lives.
The cost of the paged shape is one extra pointer hop to resolve an overflow chunk’s address (an inline chunk is a single load). We hide it by resolving a chunk’s base address once per chunk during iteration and striding within it, so the extra hop is amortized across hundreds of rows rather than paid per row.
EntityHandles — the per-row handle array — got the identical treatment: it’s a paged store using the same machinery (inline fast path included), configured so a row lands in the same chunk-and-slot as it does in every component column.
6.3 The row count becomes an atomic
Previously “how many rows does this archetype have?” was answered by EntityHandles.size(). With a paged store there is no .size(), and more importantly, the count needs to be claimable atomically. So the archetype now has an atomic RowCount.
Appending a row is now genuinely lock-free:

fetch_add(1) on RowCount — this atomically claims a unique row number. Two threads appending get two different rows, guaranteed, with no lock.
Ensure the one chunk that row lands in exists (CAS growth if needed).
Write the row.

That’s it. Two threads spawning into the same archetype now proceed in parallel: they claim different rows, touch different chunks (or the same chunk at different offsets), and never contend on a lock.

A cautionary tale. The first version of “ensure the chunk exists” re-checked every chunk from zero on every single append. That’s O(rows) work per append, i.e. O(rows²) to build an archetype. A single-entity spawn benchmark went from 23 ms to 669 ms. The fix was obvious once seen — only ensure the chunk the new row actually lands in — but it’s a good reminder that “correct” and “not accidentally quadratic” are different properties, and only benchmarking catches the difference.

6.4 What this bought, and what it cost
Bought: concurrent readers can no longer crash (nothing moves), and same-archetype spawns parallelize. Cost: a single spawn is ~25% slower than the old flat-array version, because an atomic fetch_add and a two-level pointer walk are inherently more work than a bump-and-write into a flat array. We consider that an acceptable price for the safety and parallelism — and §9 explains why we chased, but did not fully eliminate, that 25%.

7. Everything else that hangs off a spawn
Creating an entity isn’t just “append a row.” It also fires observer notifications, may add sparse components, and may set up slot hierarchies. Each needed its own concurrency story.
7.1 Observers: defer, then drain
An observer is a callback that fires when a component is added, removed, etc. Observers run user code, which is not thread-safe, and they read a shared map that isn’t safe to write from many threads.
So during a concurrent spawn, we don’t run observers and we don’t touch the map. We record each notification into a lock-free queue and move on. Later, at a single safe point on the game thread (§8), we drain the queue into the map, and observers run normally. The observers themselves never learn they were fed from multiple threads.
7.2 The finding that mattered most: batch the notifications
This is the most important performance lesson in the whole project, so it gets its own spotlight.
When we first parallelized bulk spawning, it barely got faster — roughly the same as the single-threaded path, despite using every core. We had assumed the bottleneck was a lock. It wasn’t. Careful profiling (with Tracy) showed the culprit was the observer queue itself: recording one notification per entity per component meant a 100,000-entity, 3-component spawn did 300,000 individual lock-free enqueues, and that was eating roughly half of the per-entity spawn time.
The fix was to stop enqueuing per entity. Every entity in a bulk spawn gets the same set of components, so we record one batched notification per component type that carries the whole list of entities. 300,000 enqueues became 3.
Before:  for each entity:  for each component:  enqueue(one entity)     → 300,000 enqueues
After:   for each component: enqueue(all 100,000 entities at once)       → 3 enqueues

That single change is what turned “no faster” into “2.3× faster.” The lock we’d been worried about was never the problem. Measure before you optimize is a cliché precisely because it keeps being true.
7.3 Sparse components and slots
Some component types are stored sparsely — not in the archetype’s columns, but in a side table keyed by entity, for components that only a few entities have. Slot hierarchies (parent/child relationships between an entity’s sub-parts) are implemented on top of this sparse machinery.
Two things made these concurrency-safe:

The sparse side-table’s data was made paged (same idea as §6.2), so a pointer it hands back stays valid across later concurrent additions.
Each sparse store carries a small mutex covering its bookkeeping (inserting into its map, growing its pages). This is a lock, but a narrow one, and different component types have independent locks.

The one constraint: the set of sparse stores a prefab needs is created when the prefab is baked, not during the spawn. So a worker-thread spawn only ever finds an existing store and adds to it (safe) — it never has to create one (which would mutate the shared store map). This is why prefab baking is currently game-thread-only; see Limitations.

8. The frame model: when is concurrent creation legal?
Concurrency needs a rule for when it’s allowed, or every read everywhere would need to defend against every write. Catalyst’s rule is simple and is expressed as two non-overlapping regions of a frame:
graph LR
    M["MUTATION WINDOW<br/>spawns legal (any thread)<br/>processors NOT running"] --> B{{"BARRIER"}}
    B --> P["PROCESSOR WINDOW<br/>processors run<br/>no structural mutation"]
    P --> M2["next frame's<br/>MUTATION WINDOW"]


In the mutation window, any thread may spawn.
In the processor window, the engine runs the systems that iterate entities. No structural mutation happens here (systems that want to change structure use the deferred command buffer, unchanged from before).
The barrier between them is the safe point. It does two things: it waits for every in-flight spawn to finish, and it drains the observer queue into the map. Only then do processors run.

“Waits for in-flight spawns” is implemented with a counter (ActiveSpawnCount) that each spawn bumps up while it works and drops when done. The barrier spins help-executes on other pending jobs rather than idling — until the counter hits zero. Because a spawn’s final act (dropping the counter) is ordered after all its writes, the barrier seeing zero guarantees every spawned entity is fully formed and visible before any processor reads it.
8.1 One API, main-thread aware
There is a single public Instantiate call. It doesn’t ask the caller which thread they’re on. Internally it checks a cheap thread-local flag: on the game thread it writes observer notifications straight to the map (no queue needed) and skips the spawn-counter bracket; on any other thread it takes the deferred/queued path. Callers never think about it.

9. Single vs. bulk creation
The two spawn paths share machinery but optimize for different shapes.
Single spawn (Instantiate(prefab)): claim one row, fill its columns, publish it. Optimized for latency. We spent real effort shaving its cost: fusing the index allocation with grabbing its record pointer (one lookup through the table instead of two), skipping the generation read for fresh indices (§5.5), and reusing the chunk address that “ensure the chunk exists” already computed instead of looking it up again. These took single-spawn from a regressed ~237 ns down to ~206 ns — still above the old flat-array 165 ns, because, again, the atomic claim and paged walk are irreducibly more work than a flat bump.

Not every idea paid off, and we kept the honest record. We added an approximate free-list-empty counter so allocation could skip a queue probe when there’s nothing to recycle. It’s correct and low-risk, but in our Debug benchmarks it made no measurable difference — the queue probe wasn’t as expensive as we’d guessed. It stays because it can only help under real recycling load, but we flagged it for removal if a profiled build ever shows it’s dead weight.

Bulk spawn (Instantiate(prefab, count, out)): reserve count rows with one atomic (§6.3), then fan the per-entity fill across the worker pool. The batch size handed to each worker is computed from the L1 cache size and the entity’s byte size, the same heuristic the parallel query iterator uses, so each job works on a cache-friendly slice. Above a threshold (1024 entities) it goes parallel; below, the job-dispatch overhead isn’t worth it and it fills inline. This path, with the observer batching from §7.2, is the one that’s 2.3× faster than the original serial code.

10. Reading while the world is being built
The last piece is subtle and worth its own section, because it’s the part most likely to surprise a user.
Catalyst allows ForEach (iterating entities matching a query) to be called anywhere, not just inside a system during the processor window. Editor panels, UI widgets, and tools all do it. We call these out-of-tick reads. And they can happen on a different thread while spawns are running — so an out-of-tick read can overlap the mutation window.
Two guarantees were needed.
10.1 No crashes (came for free)
Because storage is now stable-addressed (§6), an out-of-tick read cannot crash from the reallocation problem of §4. The memory it walks never moves. ForEach snapshots the row count once per archetype at the start, iterates that many rows, and every chunk it touches is stable. This guarantee fell out of the storage redesign at no extra cost.
10.2 No half-built entities (the publication pattern)
Crash-safety isn’t enough. A spawn claims its row (bumping the row count) before it fills in the data. A reader that snapshots the count at that instant would iterate a row that’s been claimed but not yet written — reading uninitialized garbage.
The fix is a classic publish-then-observe pattern:

The spawn is split into reserve (claim the row, but leave its handle slot as a null sentinel) and publish (write the handle last, after all the entity’s data is in place, with “release” memory ordering that guarantees the data writes are visible first).
Handle chunks are zero-filled on allocation, so a reserved-but-unpublished row reads a null handle — a clear “not ready” marker.
The reader skips any row whose handle is null, and uses “acquire” memory ordering so that once it sees a valid handle, the entity’s data is guaranteed visible.

sequenceDiagram
    participant W as Spawner
    participant Slot as Row
    participant R as Out-of-tick reader
    W->>Slot: reserve row (handle = null)
    W->>Slot: write component data
    W->>Slot: publish handle (release) ✅
    Note over R: reads handle (acquire)
    R->>Slot: null? → skip.  valid? → data is safe to read

There’s a wrinkle we handled: if a row was previously used, destroyed, and is now being re-reserved, its slot still holds the old handle — not null. So the destroy path nulls the vacated slot, preserving the invariant “a reserved-but-unpublished row always reads null.”
10.3 Paying nothing when nobody’s spawning
The per-row null check isn’t free, and out-of-tick reads are common — so charging every out-of-tick read for a safety check that only matters during a spawn would be a bad trade. The first implementation did exactly that and regressed iteration from 6.9 ns/row to 18.5.
The fix: the check runs only when out-of-tick AND a spawn is actually in flight (ActiveSpawnCount != 0), read after the row-count snapshot so that seeing zero provably means every counted row is published. The overwhelmingly common case — an out-of-tick read with no concurrent spawn — takes the identical fast path as in-tick iteration and pays nothing. Measured back at 6.9 ns/row, unchanged from baseline.
This is validated by a stress test that runs 100,000 concurrent spawns while a reader thread hammers ForEach in a loop: it never observes a half-built entity and never crashes.

11. Current limitations
We are deliberately honest about what this does not do.

Single-spawn is ~25% slower than the pre-concurrency flat-array path (~206 ns vs ~165 ns), but only in debug config. Once you use release config, it actually flips - single-spawn becomes about 25% faster.
Prefab baking is still game-thread-only. Baking creates archetypes and sparse stores (mutating shared maps). Instantiating a baked prefab is fully concurrent; baking one is not. In practice you bake at load time and instantiate at runtime, so this rarely bites — but it’s a real constraint, tracked as future work.
Sparse-filtered reads during spawns have a visibility nuance. An out-of-tick read that filters on a sparse component can momentarily fail to match an entity whose archetype handle is published but whose sparse element is still mid-insertion. It skips that entity (never reads garbage) — the entity simply appears one iteration late. Harmless in practice; tightenable if it ever matters.
~4.19 million entities per library. The handle table’s fixed spine caps a single library at 1024 × 4096 slots. Raising it is a one-constant change; we just haven’t needed to.
Memory overhead. Stable addressing isn’t free in memory: each column and the handle store carry a small always-paid inline chunk array (InlineChunkCount pointers, ~32 bytes at the default of 4), where the old flat array started at nearly nothing. This is deliberately tiny — an earlier design paid a ~2KB spine per column and blew up to ~1GB under archetype explosion (§6.2) — but it is still a per-column tax, so an archetype with many components and few entities pays a few dozen bytes per column it barely uses.
No concurrent destruction. By design (§2). Removing entities from multiple threads is not supported; use entity disabling.


12. Future work

Concurrent destruction. The hard part of deletion is that removing a row usually means moving the last row into the hole (a “swap-remove”), which relocates a live entity — the exact “moving data” hazard we avoided for creation. The plausible design is tombstoning: a concurrent “delete” only marks the row dead (an atomic write of the null-handle sentinel — the very check §10.2 already performs), and the actual physical compaction happens later at the barrier when no reader or spawner is live. It’s a natural extension, not a rewrite, but it wasn’t a priority.
Thread-local index blocks (a “TLAB”). Borrowed from allocator/GC terminology: instead of every thread hitting the shared index counter for one index at a time, each thread grabs a block of indices in one atomic and hands them out privately with no further atomics until the block runs dry. It would help the “many threads each spawning singles, heavily” pattern. Deferred because bulk already has its own one-atomic path, and single-spawn atomic contention hasn’t profiled as a problem. Its wrinkle is wasted indices when a block is only partly used.
Cheaper bulk chunk allocation. After the observer fix, the dominant remaining bulk cost is simply allocating the chunks, and it does not parallelize. A bulk spawn of 100 K entities allocates on the order of 3,000 small new[] chunks (the count grows with both chunk count and column count, because more components per entity means fewer entities per 16 KB chunk and more columns to allocate). Profiled on a 32-thread machine, this shows up as a serial ~2.3 ms prologue in AddEntities for a 3-component spawn — pure Amdahl serial fraction that no amount of fill parallelism can move.
We tried the obvious fix — allocate lazily inside the parallel fill, so each worker new[]s the chunks its own row-range enters. This failed, and instructively so: it did not remove the serialization, it relocated it into the heap allocator’s internal lock. The default Windows heap serializes concurrent allocations, so 32 workers hitting new[] at their chunk boundaries simultaneously pile up on that lock — whichever worker holds it shows a ~1.8 ms spike inside a single “spawn entity” zone, and the total comes out slightly worse than the serial version. The lesson: the problem was never where the allocation happens, it’s the sheer count of small allocations; no allocator, parallel or serial, scales to thousands of tiny new[]s per operation.
The viable fix is therefore to collapse the allocation count, not distribute it: for a bulk spawn of known size, allocate one large slab per column covering the whole contiguous row-range (turning ~3,000 allocations into ~4) and point the spine chunk entries into it. The serial reserve then becomes a handful of large VirtualAllocs, and page-faulting on first touch stays in the parallel fill where it scales. The real cost is on the free side: today each spine pointer is independently delete[]-able, so slab-backing requires the column to own its slabs (OwnedBlocks) with the spine entries demoted to non-owning views, and the concurrent single-spawn EnsureChunk path to register its one-chunk allocations under a light lock. A contained change to ComponentColumn, but one that touches the free path and the single-spawn hot path, so it is deliberately deferred rather than layered on.
Thread-safe baking. Making BakePrefab callable off the game thread removes the last single-thread constraint in the creation pipeline.
The job-scheduler question. Profiling surfaced occasional ~200 µs gaps at the start/end of parallel-fill jobs, and per-entity cost spikes that look like cache effects. These point at the job system and the cache-size batching heuristic rather than Catalyst storage, and are worth a separate investigation.


13. Conclusion
Concurrent entity creation in Catalyst came down to a single principle — stable addressing — applied uniformly: to the entity handle table, to archetype columns, and to the per-row handle array. Once memory never moves, appends become lock-free atomic row claims, and reads become safe by construction. Layered on top are a deferred-and-batched observer path, narrow locks for the sparse side-tables, a frame barrier that fences the mutation window from the processor window, and a publish-then-observe protocol that lets reads run concurrently with spawns without ever seeing a half-built entity.
The engineering lessons generalize beyond this feature:

Stable addressing is the enabling property for lock-free concurrency over growable data. Get that, and most of the hard parts dissolve.
Your assumed bottleneck is probably wrong. We spent effort removing a lock that was never the problem; the real cost was per-item queue traffic, invisible until profiled and obvious afterward.
“Correct” and “fast” are separate checks. A quadratic loop and a per-row safety check both passed correctness and both quietly cost an order of magnitude until measured.

The result: any thread can create an entity and use it immediately.
If you are making your own game engine, or working in an existing engine and are asking yourself: Is it worthwhile to do this?
Honestly, for performance, it’s not. Spawning entities is already blazing fast. There’s simply too many moving parts in a fully multithreadable ECS data storage which just cripples its performance.
Here is a table from a benchmark that would spawn 100k entities on Alchemy’s release config back when it was fully serial, no multithreading:



































OperationEntities/OpsTotal Time (ms)ns/OpSingle Instantiate (1 comp)1000006.74800067.480000Bulk Instantiate (1 comp)1000002.15920021.592000Bulk Instantiate (3 comp)1000003.84210038.4210006.7 milliseconds for 100k entities, and that’s the inefficient method. Bulk goes down to 2.1, and a complicated entity goes up to 3.8.
Here is the same table, but after the concurrent spawning changes:





























OperationEntities/OpsTotal Time (ms)ns/OpSingle Instantiate (1 comp)1000004.54790045.479000Bulk Instantiate (1 comp)1000001.51900015.190000Bulk Instantiate (3 comp)1000003.76470037.647000
Here’s the thing, the performance difference is actually far worse than you’d think. You would think that on my machine, a 9950x with 32 threads that this bulk parallel spawning should be at minimum 3x faster. But no, it’s actually at best 25% faster, and at worst equal. But remember, the second table has 32 threads. The first one has one. The second table should be running circles around the first table.
The good news is: §12 (Future work) has a ton of solutions to fix this horrible performance scaling.
When we look at these two tables inside a Tracy file, the bulk instantiate in the serial version was taking 1.9ms. Concurrent version was 800 μs, so about twice as fast (and that’s including the odd gaps in the job distribution), but using all 32 threads. Preparing all the chunks, handles, etc, is what eats up those 3ms and makes us match the old serial version.
Most of the world comprises of entities that only have a handful of components, like a transform and mesh component so it’s not something we’re really worried about. Most of the time that you are spawning this many entities it’s during a loading screen where you can hide most of these spikes. And even then, you can implement a frame-budgeted spawn system. You start a job that spawns X entities in Y amount of time, and only give it Z amount of time per frame.
So why did Alchemy do this? Alchemy did it primarily because of how multithreaded the entire engine is. World streaming involves spawning many entities on multiple threads, the asset registry involves spawning many entities on multiple threads, and many other sections needed to spawn entities on any thread.
For Alchemy, this was actually necessary.
But this was one of those rare circumstances where going serial to parallel actually harmed performance (the entire CPU is now required with this system to match the speed of the single thread version). Hopefully after the points found in the future-work section is tackled, we will finally have some proper numbers to be proud of.
If you are making a single game, maybe not even with a game editor, I would say; don’t bother with concurrent entity spawning. It’s a lot of work, with a lot of high-risk choices. There’s a reason why you don’t see this feature in big ECS frameworks.

Spreadsheet Generation
Crucible allows you to generate spreadsheets through libxlsxwriter, which is a library for writing .xlsx files
See Also

Crucible — the testing framework this feature is part of

Crucible/Testing-Guide.title: Testing Guide
Crucible/Testing-Guide.content:
Crucible Testing Guide
This guide is written for AI agents working in this codebase. It covers how to write, compile, run, and analyze Crucible tests. It is useful for humans, but it is primarily meant for AI agents.

Source of Truth
Before writing any tests, read the following files directly — do not rely on documentation alone, as APIs evolve:

Source/Engine/Crucible/CrucibleRegistry.h — The Spec class definition. Every test method (Describe, It, LatentIt, BeforeEach, AfterEach, Cleanup, TestEqual, TestTrue, TestFalse, SetBenchmarkColumns, AddBenchmarkRow and potentially more) is defined here with its exact signature.
Source/Engine/Crucible/Tests/CrucibleTests.cpp — Canonical simple examples. This file tests Crucible itself, so it’s always kept minimal and up to date. For more complex tests, search for more specs within the codebase.


Naming Convention
Test spec names follow a dot-separated hierarchy. Always use at least three sections.
Engine.<System>.<Feature>


The first segment is always Engine for engine-level tests
The second is the system being tested (e.g., Catalyst, Tags, Jobs)
The third is the specific feature or behavior (e.g., Entity Query, Hierarchy)
Additional segments are allowed for specs that cover multiple sub-features (e.g., Engine.Crucible.Example.LatentTest)
Three segments is preferred — only go deeper when there is a genuine reason

Examples:
Engine.Catalyst.Entity Query
Engine.Tags.Hierarchy
Engine.Jobs.Parallel Execution
Engine.Crucible.Example.LatentTest


Writing Tests
File Placement
Tests are co-located with their module:
Source/Engine/<Module>/Tests/

For example, Catalyst tests go in Source/Engine/Catalyst/Tests/.
All test code must be wrapped in #if ALCHEMY_WITH_CRUCIBLE.
Structure
#if ALCHEMY_WITH_CRUCIBLE
 
BEGIN_SPEC(MyFeatureSpec, "Engine.MySystem.My Feature")
    // Declare any member variables shared across tests here
    int Counter = 0;
END_SPEC(MyFeatureSpec)
 
void MyFeatureSpec::Define()
{
    Describe("Behavior group", [this]()
    {
        BeforeEach([this]()
        {
            Counter = 0;
        });
 
        It("should do something", [this]()
        {
            Counter++;
            TestEqual("Counter incremented", Counter, 1);
        });
    });
}
 
#endif
For the full API (all method signatures, LatentIt, Cleanup, benchmark methods), read the Spec class in Source/Engine/Crucible/CrucibleRegistry.h directly.

Spec Versioning
Every spec carries a version number, passed as the optional third argument to BEGIN_SPEC. Omit it and the spec is version 1.
BEGIN_SPEC(ArchetypeSpec, "Engine.Catalyst.Archetypes", 3)
END_SPEC(ArchetypeSpec)
Changing an existing spec means bumping its version, whenever the change makes its results no longer comparable to earlier runs: a meaningful refactor of what the spec exercises, or a correction to an assertion or a benchmark that turned out to be flawed. The version is what lets anyone comparing two reports see whether both came from the same spec definition, instead of silently comparing numbers produced by different logic.
A bump must say why. Record the version number, your username, and the reason, directly above BEGIN_SPEC:
/**Version 2: V: Bumped from version 1 due to a flawed test
 * Version 3: V: Massive architecture change, caused some of the
 * API to change and thus, tests had to be changed */
BEGIN_SPEC(ArchetypeSpec, "Engine.Catalyst.Archetypes", 3)
The version records that a spec changed, never what changed — so a bump with no explanation is worse than no bump at all, because it signals a difference without ever saying what it was. Version 1 needs no comment; it is the base.
The version surfaces on the Running spec: banner, on every FAILED line, on each log.txt spec header, in the Version column of Report.xlsx, and in each benchmark markdown title. See Spec versioning for the full rules.

Compiling
Run from the engine root:
BuildProject.bat

This compiles a Debug build. Tests are included automatically in Debug and RelWithDebInfo builds via ALCHEMY_WITH_CRUCIBLE=1.

Running Tests
# Run one spec
Build/bin/Debug/AlchemyRunner.exe -RunTest="Engine.Tags"
 
# Run multiple specs (comma-separated)
Build/bin/Debug/AlchemyRunner.exe -RunTest="Engine.Tags,Engine.Jobs"
 
# Run every spec — a bare -RunTest carries no filter, and an empty filter matches everything
Build/bin/Debug/AlchemyRunner.exe -RunTest
 
# Run all specs via CTest (one process per spec)
ctest -C Debug --output-on-failure
-RunTest has to be present for any spec to run at all. AlchemyRunner.exe with no arguments starts the engine and runs nothing.
Headless Mode
-Headless runs without a window, a swapchain or a GPU device: the windowing, rendering and UI subsystems decline construction, and the engine drives a windowless tick loop. Use it for CI, and for running a suite in the background while you keep working.
Build/bin/Debug/AlchemyRunner.exe -RunTest -Headless
Headless means no window, not no GPU — the two are independent. A spec that needs the renderer still cannot run here, because -Headless skips the device entirely today. Ask for the subsystem rather than assuming it:
auto* Render = Engine::Get()->GetSubsystem<RenderSubsystem>();
if(!Render)
{
    TestTrue("Skipped: no renderer in a headless run", true);
    return;
}
Latent tests are unaffected: the headless loop takes its delta time from a steady clock instead of ImGui, so SetTimeout behaves as it does in a windowed run.
Worker Count
-Workers=N pins the job system’s pool, which otherwise sizes itself to the host’s cores minus one. A two-core machine yields a single worker, so a spec that wants to watch two jobs run at once sees nothing. Asking for more workers than cores oversubscribes them and the scheduling paths still run, time-sliced:
Build/bin/Debug/AlchemyRunner.exe -RunTest -Headless -Workers=4
Check JobSubsystem::GetWorkerCount() before asserting on parallelism, and skip when the pool holds one worker. Assert unconditionally and the spec reports a failure on a small host for a job system that is working correctly. Guard before building fixtures, not after: a heavyweight setup that only then discovers it cannot observe concurrency may exhaust the latent timeout and report a timeout rather than a skip.
Recursive Filters with *
A filter is matched positionally, one hierarchy level at a time. Each * in the filter matches exactly one level. This is the correct way to run “all tests under a system” — there is no implicit prefix match, so a bare -RunTest="Engine.Catalyst" matches only a spec named exactly Engine.Catalyst (which usually does not exist) and runs nothing.
# WRONG: matches only a spec literally named "Engine.Catalyst" — in this case, there's no test just named "Engine.Catalyst". Runs nothing
Build/bin/Debug/AlchemyRunner.exe -RunTest="Engine.Catalyst"
 
# Every spec exactly one level below Engine.Catalyst
# (Engine.Catalyst.EntityQuery, Engine.Catalyst.Processor, ...)
Build/bin/Debug/AlchemyRunner.exe -RunTest="Engine.Catalyst.*"
 
# Two levels deep (e.g. Engine.Crucible.Example.LatentTest)
Build/bin/Debug/AlchemyRunner.exe -RunTest="Engine.Crucible.*.*"
 
# Wildcards may sit anywhere: every "Benchmark" spec across all systems
Build/bin/Debug/AlchemyRunner.exe -RunTest="Engine.*.Benchmark"
Rules:

Each * is exactly one level — Engine.Catalyst.* does not reach Engine.Catalyst.Foo.Bar; use Engine.Catalyst.*.* for that.
The filter and the spec name must have the same number of segments for a match. A * never spans multiple levels.
A filter with no * is an exact-name match, exactly as before.
Wildcard filters combine with commas like any other: -RunTest="Engine.Catalyst.*,Engine.Tags.*".

In the Frontend, the Recursive Depth (*) field (above “Test Discovery Status”) appends that many * levels to every checked spec. Check a category node such as Engine.Catalyst, set the depth to 1, and it runs Engine.Catalyst.*.
Exit codes:

0 — All tests passed
1 — At least one test failed
-1 — Engine crashed during startup



                  
                  Crash Detection 
                  
                

At the moment, the engine does not report whether it has crashed. Meaning LLMs will often see an output log that isn’t finished. If all tests have passed by the time the crash happens, they will often think “all tests passed”. In order to evaluate whether the engine crashed or not, all LLMs need to verify that [AlchemyEngineCore] Engine Shutdown Complete. actually appears in the output.



Reading Results
Report Location
Saved/Crucible/Automation/{YYYY-MM-DD-HH-MM}/
├── Report.xlsx              (benchmark tables + per-spec logs — for human review)
├── log.txt                  (test pass/fail output — use this as an agent)
└── Benchmarks/              (only created if any spec had benchmark data)
    ├── Engine.Catalyst.Benchmark.md
    └── Engine.Jobs.Benchmark.md

log.txt Format
The log is split into named sections, one per spec. Here is the actual format from a real run:
--- System Log ---
[AlchemyEngineCore] Subsystem Startup: AutomationSubsystem
...

--- Engine.Crucible.Example ---
[AlchemyEngineCore] [Crucible] START: Engine.Crucible.Example.Math.should add numbers correctly
[AlchemyEngineCore] [Crucible] PASSED: Engine.Crucible.Example.Math.should add numbers correctly
[AlchemyEngineCore] [Crucible] START: Engine.Crucible.Example.Math.should fail on purpose to test reporting
[AlchemyEngineCore] [Crucible] FAILED: Engine.Crucible.Example.Math.should fail on purpose to test reporting
[AlchemyEngineCore]   Reason: Failed: This is a forced failure
[AlchemyEngineCore] [Crucible] START: Engine.Crucible.Example.Logging.should fail the test when an error is logged
[AlchemyEngineCore] Forced log error to verify Crucible integration
[AlchemyEngineCore] [Crucible] FAILED: Engine.Crucible.Example.Logging.should fail the test when an error is logged
[AlchemyEngineCore]   Reason: [AlchemyEngineCore] Forced log error to verify Crucible integration

--- Engine.Crucible.Lifecycle ---
[AlchemyEngineCore] [Crucible] START: Engine.Crucible.Lifecycle.Setup and Teardown.should run test 1
[AlchemyEngineCore] [Crucible] PASSED: Engine.Crucible.Lifecycle.Setup and Teardown.should run test 1

Parsing rules:

--- SpecName --- marks the start of a spec’s section
[Crucible] START: <full test name> — test has begun
[Crucible] PASSED: <full test name> — test passed
[Crucible] FAILED: <full test name> — test failed
  Reason: <message> lines immediately after FAILED explain why
Any [Crucible] FAILED line in a spec’s section means that spec failed

Report.xlsx
This file is for human review only. AI agents cannot read .xlsx files. Use log.txt for pass/fail analysis and the Benchmarks/ markdown files for performance data.

Expected Errors — Muting with ScopedLogMute
By default a LogError during a test fails that test (see the log.txt example above). But some tests deliberately exercise error paths — validating that bad input is rejected, that an invalid state is detected, or that Crucible itself reports failures. In those tests the LogError is expected, and left unmuted it produces two problems:

It wrongly fails the test, because Crucible treats any LogLevel::Error as a failure.
It paints red in the output log, making it hard to tell a genuine failure from an intentional one.

Wrap the region that is expected to error in a ScopedLogMute (from Engine/Logging/Log.h). While muted, errors/warnings neither paint the console nor fail the test — but they are still recorded in the report.
#include "Engine/Logging/Log.h"
 
It("should reject invalid input without failing the test", [this]()
{
    Alchemy::ScopedLogMute Mute; /**The LogError below is expected — don't fail this test*/
 
    String Result = ParsePath("<<< not a path >>>");
    TestTrue("Invalid path returns empty", Result.IsEmpty());
    // ParsePath logged an error internally; muted, so it neither paints red nor fails us
});
Keep the mute scope as tight as possible — mute only the call you expect to error, so a genuine, unexpected error elsewhere in the test still fails it:
It("should still catch unexpected errors", [this]()
{
    {
        Alchemy::ScopedLogMute Mute;
        ProvokeExpectedError();      // expected — muted
    }
    DoSomethingThatShouldNotError(); // if THIS errors, the test still fails
});
The mute is nestable and can also be driven manually with PushLogMute() / PopLogMute() when a scope guard doesn’t fit (always pair them). See Logging for the full API and behavior. Note the switch is global, not per-thread, so errors from background jobs spawned during the muted scope are muted too.
Benchmarks
Writing Benchmarks
Read Source/Engine/Crucible/CrucibleRegistry.h for the exact SetBenchmarkColumns and AddBenchmarkRow signatures. Values are always passed as strings.
Describe("Performance", [this]()
{
    SetBenchmarkColumns({"Operation", "Iterations", "Time (ms)", "ns/Op"});
 
    It("Benchmark: Entity query", [this]()
    {
        // ... run timed loop ...
        AddBenchmarkRow("ForEach (uncached)", {"100000", "12.4", "124"});
        AddBenchmarkRow("ForEach (cached)",   {"100000", "1.8",  "18"});
    });
});
Evaluating Benchmark Numbers
Always check computer specs before drawing conclusions. The numbers produced by this machine are not universally valid. Ask the user what CPU and RAM they are running on, or check using system tools:
# Windows
wmic cpu get name
wmic memorychip get capacity
Context matters: a result of 50 ns/Op on a high-end desktop is very different from the same result on a laptop or CI server.
Small fluctuations (~5%) are normal. The machine running tests may have background processes active (IDE, browser, system services). Do not treat a 3–5% change as a regression unless the user has confirmed no background load.
If something is unexpectedly slow, do not guess the cause. Enable Tracy profiling and capture a trace:


Tracy is already integrated (see Source/Engine/Subsystems/AutomationSubsystem.h and the Tracy documentation in Profiler)


Pass -CaptureTrace to the Runner to record a trace headlessly, no Frontend or live Tracy UI needed:
Build/bin/RelWithDebInfo/AlchemyRunner.exe -RunTest="Engine.Catalyst.*" -CaptureTrace
The Runner spawns Tracy’s capture.exe against its own Tracy client and writes a timestamped
Profile-{YYYY-MM-DD-HH-MM-SS}.tracy to Saved/Crucible/Traces/. The file is flushed when the run
exits. Combine with -Headless for a background capture. Archive the .tracy alongside the run’s
report (see Tracking Performance During a Session) so a before/after pair can be compared in the
Tracy UI. Capture in RelWithDebInfo — Debug numbers are unreliable (see below).
Keep in mind that capturing a trace will impact performance. In general, you can expect a 2x performance decrease while capturing a trace.


Always use Release or RelWithDebInfo configurations when measuring performance. Debug mode has consistently given us unreliable numbers. For example, Catalyst went through numerous changes to enable concurrent entity spawning, and it was proving to be 4x faster. But that was in debug config. Once we switched to RelWithDebInfo, all the performance gains vanished.
Tracking Performance During a Session
When working on a performance improvement task, archive each benchmark run so you can compare before and after your changes.
Archive a run:

After tests complete, the report lands in Saved/Crucible/Automation/{timestamp}/
Copy it to Saved/Crucible/Sessions/{session-name}/{timestamp}/

Sessions/ is persistent — Automation/ is frequently cleared


Create a RunNotes.md file alongside the copied report describing what the code looked like at that point:

Saved/Crucible/Sessions/archetype-storage-rework/
├── 2026-03-21-14-00/
│   ├── Report.xlsx
│   ├── log.txt
│   └── RunNotes.md       ← describe what was changed before this run
└── 2026-03-21-15-30/
    ├── Report.xlsx
    ├── log.txt
    └── RunNotes.md

RunNotes.md format:
## Run: 2026-03-21 14:00
 
**Description:** Baseline before reworking archetype component storage.
Components stored in separate per-type arrays. No column caching.
 
**Key metrics to watch:**
- Engine.Catalyst.Benchmark — ForEach ns/Op
Comparing runs:
Ask the user to read out the relevant numbers from Report.xlsx (since you cannot read xlsx), then calculate the delta:
Old: 169 ns/Op
New: 124 ns/Op
Change: (124 - 169) / 169 = -26.6% → IMPROVEMENT

Treat changes under ±5% as noise unless background load is confirmed to be zero. Flag anything over 10% as worth investigating.
See Also

Crucible — overview and creating tests
Spreadsheet Generation — writing benchmark results to .xlsx
Profiler — Tracy profiling during benchmark tests

A Note on LogWarning
LogWarning() does not fail a test. However, be aware that a future flag will treat warnings as errors (similar to -Werror in compiler flags). This is intentional — warning accumulation is a known problem in large codebases, and studios routinely enforce zero-warning policies. Write tests that do not produce warnings unless the warning is specifically what is being tested. When a warning is expected, wrap it in a ScopedLogMute (see Expected Errors above) — the mute covers both warnings and errors.

Input/Input-Handlers.title: Input Handlers

Input/Input-Profiles.title: Input Profiles
Input/Input-Profiles.content:
Input Profiles
An InputProfile owns a set of named InputActions. Each action maps one or more physical inputs (keys, axes, buttons) to a logical name that game code queries.

If you’re coming from Unreal Engine, a good way to think about profiles is that it’s like a Input Mapping Context, but the Input Action’s are defined inside of it. You do not create separate Input Action assets.

Profiles are defined once and shared across entities by pointer. All soldiers, all vehicles, all UI cursors can reference the same profile object.
Defining a Profile
InputProfile PlayerProfile;
 
PlayerProfile.Action("Jump")
    .Key(Keys::Space, InputTrigger::OnPressed)
    .GamepadButton(Gamepad::Button::FaceBottom, InputTrigger::OnPressed);
 
PlayerProfile.Action("Move")
    .Composite2D(Keys::W, Keys::S, Keys::A, Keys::D)
    .GamepadAxis2D(Gamepad::Axis::LeftX, Gamepad::Axis::LeftY)
    .Modifier(std::make_unique<DeadZoneModifier>(0.1f));
 
PlayerProfile.Action("Look")
    .MouseDelta()
    .GamepadAxis2D(Gamepad::Axis::RightX, Gamepad::Axis::RightY)
    .Modifier(std::make_unique<DeadZoneModifier>(0.05f))
    .Modifier(std::make_unique<SensitivityModifier>(2.5f));
Binding an Entity to a Profile
Add InputReceiverComponent (what device feeds this entity) and one or more InputProfileComponent slots (which profile to apply).
// DeviceID: 0=keyboard, 1=mouse, 2+=gamepad slot
Library.AddComponent<InputReceiverComponent>(Player, {-1}); // -1 = any device
Library.AddComponent<InputStateComponent>(Player);
Library.AddComponent<InputProfileComponent>(Player, {&PlayerProfile}, "Default");
Multiple Profile Slots (Context Switching)
Entities can carry multiple profile slots that can be enabled/disabled via Library.EnableComponent() and Library.DisableComponent(). The slot name is the identifier — it answers “are you in the vehicle context?“.
// Add both profiles; only Default is active initially
Library.AddComponent<InputProfileComponent>(Player, {&FootProfile}, "Default");
Library.AddComponent<InputProfileComponent>(Player, {&VehicleProfile}, "Vehicle");
 
// Entering a vehicle — swap active profile
Library.DisableComponent<InputProfileComponent>(Player, "Default");
Library.EnableComponent<InputProfileComponent>(Player, "Vehicle");
 
// Check context from another system
bool InVehicle = !Library.IsComponentDisabled<InputProfileComponent>(Player, "Vehicle");
The router processes all enabled slots and merges their resolved actions into InputStateComponent each frame.

For more information about Component Slots, see Components.

Triggers





















TriggerFires whenInputTrigger::HeldEvery frame the key is held (default)InputTrigger::OnPressedFirst frame the key goes downInputTrigger::OnReleasedFirst frame the key goes up
Profile.Action("Fire")
    .Key(Keys::LeftControl, InputTrigger::Held);       // Fires every frame while held
 
Profile.Action("Reload")
    .Key(Keys::R, InputTrigger::OnPressed);             // Fires once on press
 
Profile.Action("Drop")
    .Key(Keys::G, InputTrigger::OnReleased);            // Fires once on release
Device IDs





























IDDevice0Keyboard1Mouse2Gamepad slot 0 (first controller)3Gamepad slot 1-1Any (single-player convenience)
Modifiers
Modifiers are applied in order to the resolved value each frame, after the raw value is read.

































ModifierPurposeDeadZoneModifier(Low, High=1.0f)Ignore values below Low; remap [Low, High] → [0, 1]SensitivityModifier(Scale)Multiply value or axis by a scale factorNegateModifierFlip the signNormalizeModifierClamp 2D axis to unit circle (fixes diagonal speed)ResponseCurveModifier(Exponent)Exponential response curveSmoothModifier(Time)Lerp toward target over time (stateful)
Modifiers are chained. The output of one becomes the input of the next:
Raw value 0.8f
    → DeadZoneModifier(0.1f)     →  0.78f   (remapped)
    → SensitivityModifier(1.5f)  →  1.17f   (scaled)
    → final value: 1.17f

See Also

Input State Component — the resolved per-frame state profiles write to
Input Handlers — the router processor that applies profiles to entities
Components — component slots, used by InputProfileComponent

Input/Input-State-Component.title: Input State Component
Input/Input-State-Component.content:
Input State Component
InputStateComponent is where resolved input lives each frame. Game code always reads from here — never from InputSubsystem directly.
This design makes player and AI code identical: a MovementProcessor reads InputStateComponent the same way regardless of whether the entity is controlled by a human or an AI brain.
Querying State
// Digital — button / key
bool  Pressed  = State.IsPressed ("Jump");   // true only on first frame
bool  Held     = State.IsHeld    ("Jump");   // true every frame while held
bool  Released = State.IsReleased("Jump");   // true only on first frame of release
 
// 1D analog — trigger, scroll wheel
float Value    = State.GetValue  ("Throttle"); // 0.0 → 1.0
 
// 2D analog — stick, WASD composite, mouse delta
Vec2  Move     = State.GetAxis2D ("Move");     // [-1, 1] on each axis
Per-Frame Lifecycle
Frame N:
  InputSubsystem::Tick()
      1. InputRouterHandler clears all InputStateComponents
      2. Router resolves bindings → writes new state
      3. Game processors read InputStateComponent
  End of frame
      4. JustPressed / JustReleased flags are cleared for next frame

IsHeld remains true every frame the key is down. IsPressed and IsReleased are true for exactly one frame.
AI Injection
AI processors write directly to InputStateComponent, bypassing the entire input chain. The MovementProcessor cannot tell the difference.
// Inside an AI processor
Context.Library.ForEach(Query, [](AIBrainComponent& Brain, InputStateComponent& Input)
{
    Input.SetAxis2D("Move", Brain.GetDesiredMovement());
 
    if(Brain.ShouldJump())
        Input.SetPressed("Jump");
});
This is intentional — it means any gameplay system that works for players automatically works for AI.
Components Overview





















ComponentPurposeInputStateComponentResolved per-frame input state. Read by game processors.InputReceiverComponentMarks entity as a hardware receiver. Specifies which DeviceID feeds it.InputProfileComponentOne profile slot (slotted — multiple per entity). Use Library.EnableComponent()/DisableComponent() to toggle.
See Also

Input — the full pipeline that produces this component’s state
Input Profiles — profiles define what actions are resolved into this component

Input/Input.title: Input
Input/Input.content:
Input
The input system routes platform hardware events to game entities through a processor chain. Game code never reads raw input — it always reads from InputStateComponent on entities. This makes player and AI code identical.
How it Works
GLFW Callbacks (keyboard, mouse, gamepad)
    ↓  Array<InputEvent>  (collected each frame)
InputSubsystem::Tick()
    ↓
IInputProcessor chain  (sorted by Priority, high → low)
    ├── [200+]  Custom editor/tool processors
    ├── [100]   UIInputProcessor       — consumes events ImGui wants
    └── [  0]   InputRouterProcessor   — routes remaining events to entities
                        ↓  queries all EntityLibraries
            Entities with InputReceiverComponent
                      + InputProfileComponent slots
                        ↓  writes resolved state to
                InputStateComponent   ←── AI also writes here directly

Game processors (movement, abilities, etc.) read InputStateComponent. They never interact with raw input.
Reading Input in a Processor
Context.Library.ForEach(Query, [](InputStateComponent& Input, TransformComponent& Transform)
{
    if(Input.IsPressed("Jump"))
        Transform.Position.y += 5.0f;
 
    Vec2 Move = Input.GetAxis2D("Move");
    Transform.Position += Vec3(Move.x, 0, Move.y) * Context.DeltaTime;
});
See Input Handlers, Input Profiles, Input State Component, and Key Reference for full documentation on each part of the system.
See Also

Input Handlers — the processor chain that routes and consumes events
Input Profiles — binding physical inputs to logical action names
Input State Component — the per-entity resolved input state game code reads

Input/Key-Reference.title: Key Reference
Input/Key-Reference.content:
Key Reference
Raw integer key codes are never used directly. All physical inputs are referenced through named constants in four namespaces. GLFW constants are an implementation detail inside InputEvent.h and are not exposed to game code.
Keyboard — Keys::
// Letters
Keys::A  Keys::B  Keys::C  ...  Keys::Z
 
// Numbers (top row)
Keys::Num0  Keys::Num1  ...  Keys::Num9
 
// Function keys
Keys::F1  Keys::F2  ...  Keys::F12
 
// Navigation
Keys::Space     Keys::Enter     Keys::Escape
Keys::Tab       Keys::Backspace Keys::Delete
Keys::Up        Keys::Down      Keys::Left    Keys::Right
Keys::Home      Keys::End       Keys::PageUp  Keys::PageDown
Keys::Insert
 
// Modifiers
Keys::LeftShift   Keys::RightShift
Keys::LeftCtrl    Keys::RightCtrl
Keys::LeftAlt     Keys::RightAlt
Keys::LeftSuper   Keys::RightSuper
 
// Numpad
Keys::KP0 ... Keys::KP9
Keys::KPAdd  Keys::KPSubtract  Keys::KPMultiply  Keys::KPDivide
Keys::KPEnter  Keys::KPDecimal
 
// Punctuation
Keys::Comma       Keys::Period      Keys::Slash
Keys::Semicolon   Keys::Apostrophe  Keys::Backslash
Keys::LeftBracket Keys::RightBracket Keys::GraveAccent
Keys::Minus       Keys::Equal
Mouse — MouseButton::
MouseButton::Left
MouseButton::Right
MouseButton::Middle
MouseButton::Button4   // side buttons
MouseButton::Button5
Gamepad Buttons — Gamepad::Button::
Face buttons         Stick clicks       Shoulder / trigger
──────────────       ───────────        ──────────────────
FaceBottom  (A/×)    LeftThumb          LeftBumper
FaceRight   (B/○)    RightThumb         RightBumper
FaceLeft    (X/□)                       LeftTrigger   (digital)
FaceTop     (Y/△)    D-pad              RightTrigger  (digital)
                     DpadUp
Start / Back         DpadDown           System
────────────         DpadLeft           ──────
Start                DpadRight          Guide
Back

For analog trigger values, use Gamepad::Axis::LeftTrigger / RightTrigger instead.
Gamepad Axes — Gamepad::Axis::
Gamepad::Axis::LeftX        // Left stick horizontal  [-1, 1]
Gamepad::Axis::LeftY        // Left stick vertical    [-1, 1]
Gamepad::Axis::RightX       // Right stick horizontal [-1, 1]
Gamepad::Axis::RightY       // Right stick vertical   [-1, 1]
Gamepad::Axis::LeftTrigger  // Left trigger           [ 0, 1]
Gamepad::Axis::RightTrigger // Right trigger          [ 0, 1]
Usage in Profiles
Profile.Action("Move")
    .Composite2D(Keys::W, Keys::S, Keys::A, Keys::D)         // WASD
    .GamepadAxis2D(Gamepad::Axis::LeftX, Gamepad::Axis::LeftY); // or left stick
 
Profile.Action("Shoot")
    .Key(Keys::LeftCtrl,                  InputTrigger::Held)
    .MouseButton(MouseButton::Left,        InputTrigger::Held)
    .GamepadButton(Gamepad::Button::RightBumper, InputTrigger::Held);
