Objects/Objects.title: Objects
Objects/Objects.content:
Object System
The Object System is the foundational memory and referencing architecture of the Alchemy Engine. Unlike older monolithic engines that rely on global garbage collectors, Alchemy uses a highly optimized, lock-free Intrusive Reference Counting and Handle-Based Weak Referencing system.
This allows thousands of threads to allocate, reference, and destroy objects simultaneously with zero mutex contention and zero garbage collection hitches.
The Base Object
Object is the fundamental base class for game logic elements.
It operates at exactly 48 bytes and contains:

vptr (8 bytes)
Owner (8 bytes)
ObjectHandle (8 bytes)
Guid (16 bytes)
RefCount (4 bytes)
ObjectFlags (4 bytes)

Objects are entirely responsible for their own lifetime via their RefCount. When the reference count drops to 0, the object destroys itself instantaneously.
Pointer Types
The engine provides three distinct smart pointers to interact with objects safely. Never use raw pointers for object ownership.
1. ObjectPtr<T> (Strong Pointer)
The standard strong pointer. Holding an ObjectPtr increments the internal RefCount of the object, mathematically guaranteeing it cannot be destroyed while you hold the pointer.

Cache Penalty: 0 (Direct raw pointer access)
Performance: Extremely fast. Use this in tight loops and performance-critical code.
Rules: You must be careful to avoid cyclic references (e.g., Object A holds a strong pointer to Object B, and Object B holds a strong pointer to Object A), which will cause memory leaks.

2. WeakObjectPtr<T> (Handle Pointer)
A weak pointer that does not increment the reference count. If the object dies, the weak pointer will safely return nullptr instead of crashing.

How it works: It holds an 8-byte ObjectHandle (32-bit Index, 32-bit Generation). When you call Get(), it looks up the flat array in the ObjectManager to ensure the object hasn’t been destroyed and the slot hasn’t been reused.
Performance: Requires an O(1) array lookup and generation check. Extremely fast, but not “free”.
Usage: Use for high-level gameplay logic (e.g., tracking a target, UI references, parent/child safety links).

3. SoftAssetPtr<T> (Asset Reference)
Used strictly for referencing assets on disk (e.g., /Content/Meshes/Player.alchemyasset).

It holds a string path to an asset that may or may not be loaded in memory.
It integrates with the asynchronous streaming system to load assets on demand.

The ObjectManager
The ObjectManager is a global singleton responsible for routing ObjectHandle lookups for Weak Pointers.
To ensure maximum multi-threading performance, it is completely Lock-Free.

Chunked Array: It uses an array of pointers to memory blocks (Chunks of 65,536 slots). This prevents the engine from ever having to resize or copy massive arrays, eliminating lag spikes.
Lock-Free Spawning: Threads claim new array indices using fetch_add or by popping from a moodycamel::ConcurrentQueue recycle bin.
Compare-And-Swap Expansion: If the chunked array needs to expand, threads race to allocate the new chunk using atomic CAS. No mutexes are ever used.

Spawning Objects
Always spawn objects using the NewObject<T> template. This properly initializes the RefCount, generates the serialization GUID, and registers the object with the lock-free ObjectManager to receive its Handle.
// Correct: RefCount starts at 1
ObjectPtr<MyObjectClass> Object = NewObject<MyObjectClass>(this);
 
// INCORRECT: Will not register the handle, and will leak memory!
MyObjectClass* Object = new MyObjectClass();
Serialization
Objects serialize through ObjectSerializer (Engine/Object/ObjectSerialization.h), which reads and writes whole sets of objects at once. An object persists as its dynamic (most-derived) reflected type identity, its Guid, its persistent flags, its owner (by Guid), and every reflected field it carries. The field data itself comes from the reflection system: each reflected type registers a TypeInfo::Serialize function, and ObjectSerializer invokes it for every level of the object’s inheritance chain, so a field declared on a base class is saved and restored alongside the derived type’s own fields.
References resolve by GUID, never by pointer
Because ObjectPtr and WeakObjectPtr store a raw pointer and a runtime ObjectHandle respectively, neither of which survives a save/load, both serialize as the Guid of the object they target. On save, the pointer writes its target’s Guid (an all-zero Guid for a null pointer). On load, it reads that Guid and asks the Archive’s installed IObjectResolver to turn it back into a live pointer. A weak pointer additionally rebuilds its handle from the resolved object; a strong pointer takes a fresh reference. A Guid the resolver does not recognise — a reference to an object outside the saved set — restores as null rather than dangling.
Loading is ordering-independent
ObjectSerializer writes the identity of every object as one contiguous table, followed by the field data. This lets the loader run two passes over the archive:

The first pass reads the identity table and allocates every object — assigning each its saved Guid, a fresh ObjectManager handle, and an entry in the ObjectResolver — before a single field is read.
The second pass reads the field data with the resolver installed, so any ObjectPtr/WeakObjectPtr field (and the owner link) resolves its stored Guid against objects that are all guaranteed to already exist.

The order objects appear in the saved set is therefore irrelevant. If ObjectA references ObjectB and ObjectA happens to be allocated first, ObjectB still exists by the time ObjectA’s fields are read, because no field is read until the whole set has been allocated. A reference is only ever read during the second pass — the field serializers never dereference a target while allocating — so a not-yet-constructed target can never be observed.
Ownership of the loaded graph
ObjectSerializer::LoadObjects returns the reconstructed objects as an Array<ObjectPtr<Object>>. Those strong pointers own the loaded graph: the objects live exactly as long as the caller keeps the returned pointers (or copies of them), and dropping them all destroys the graph through the normal reference-counting path.

Plugins.title: Plugins

Profiler.title: Profiler
Profiler.content:
Profiler
Tracy is the profiler of choice for Alchemy Engine. It’s open-source and incredibly powerful.
This documentation does not cover how to use Tracy. Refer to its documentation for that.
This documentation will cover how Alchemy Engine utilizes Tracy.
Ports
In order for Tracy to automatically pick up certain instances instantly, we use custom ports for the IP address you put into the connection window.

8086 is the default port  uses. The editor uses this port.
8087 is used by the frontend application. This is rarely used.
8088 is the port used by the ran application from the frontend. This means if you are performing tests, you can add :8088 to the IP address, click “Connect” and then when your tests are ran, you will automatically profile it. This is important for instantaneous test environments.

Performance notes

Keep in mind that ZoneScope and all of Tracy’s macros do incur a tiny overhead. This is important to keep in mind when doing heavy performance tests. For accurate numbers, either include Tracy macros in all of your tests, or none. Don’t mix them. But for the absolute raw numbers, you will want to exclude Tracy.

Profiling and testing with AI
AI agents are fantastic in many ways. The benchmarking feature is one of the simplest and best ways to communicate to an AI agent what is slow and what is fast.
BUT… AI likes to cheat and on top of that, it wants to give you the results you want. You can not blindly trust that the AI that you’re talking with will not make changes or suggestions that benefit nothing BUT the benchmark.
This actually happened during the development of the job system. Research was being made on how to improve the thread communication and job dispatching. What the agent implemented was code that FORCED all threads to be on at all times, pinning the CPU at max usage, minus the game thread.
This “technically” was a slight performance improvement, due to threads being able to respond nearly instantly. But it was awful for real life usage.
You must also be cautious of any benchmark test that an AI creates. They will often try to cheat in very subtle ways by modifying how the benchmark is measured.
On the other hand, they can also mess it up. In some benchmarks, I had an AI put the start of a latent test before the test actually began, then ended the measurement on the next tick. This measurement now included metrics outside of the actual test, plus the time it took to tick the automation system.
AI is great for generating tests for your systems. You can just write your code, then tell an AI agent to create a test for it. Not everyone enjoys making tests. But what you must validate is the quality of the test. Since AI doesn’t want you to be unhappy, they will often be very lazy on the quality of the test and not create very thorough tests.
For example; the first iteration of the command buffer tests would call AddComponent, then playback the commands, then check if the component existed. Which is fine, but it didn’t check whether GetComponent before calling Playback().
See Also

Jobs — job system performance is a primary profiling target
Testing Guide — how to use Tracy during automated test benchmarks

Property-Attributes/Config-Attribute.title: Config Attribute
Property-Attributes/Config-Attribute.content:
The config system persists individual object fields to human-editable .ini files. You opt a field in with a single property attribute — [[LocalConfig("File")]] or [[GlobalConfig("File")]] — and the engine handles loading on spawn, delta-only saving, the cascade of override layers, and the text encoding of the value.
class [[Reflect]] EditorCameraProcessor : public CatalystProcessor
{
    [[LocalConfig("EditorCamera")]] 
    float ScrollSpeed   = 5.0f;
    
    [[LocalConfig("EditorCamera")]] 
    float RotationSpeed = 0.3f;
};
The first time this processor is spawned through [[Objects|NewObject]], any persisted values are applied automatically. Call SaveConfig() to write changes back.
The two attributes


























AttributeScopeLives inSource-controlled?Use for[[GlobalConfig("File")]]GlobalProjectDir/Config/File.iniYes — team-sharedGameplay/system settings the whole team shares[[LocalConfig("File")]]LocalProjectDir/Saved/Config/File.iniNo — per-machineEditor preferences, keybindings, local tweaks
The "File" argument is the .ini filename (without extension). Many fields — across many types — can target the same file.

In launcher/test mode, when no project is loaded, both scopes fall back to EngineDir/Saved/Config so the store is still usable.

How a field maps to the file
Each type gets its own section, named from its module-qualified identity:
/Class/<Module>/<TypeName>

The field’s own name is the key. So EditorCameraProcessor::ScrollSpeed above persists as:
[/Class/AlchemyEditor/EditorCameraProcessor]
ScrollSpeed=8.0
Lifecycle: when load and save happen

Load is automatic. NewObject<T>() calls Object::LoadConfig() immediately after construction (the Alchemy equivalent of Unreal applying config in FObjectInitializer::PostConstructInit). It runs after the constructor on purpose — during base construction typeid resolves to the base, not the dynamic type, so config fields would be missed. Types with no config fields anywhere in their chain fast-return, so the common spawn pays only a cached check.
Save is explicit. Call Object::SaveConfig() when you want to persist the object’s current values (e.g. when the user closes a settings panel). Then ConfigStore::Flush writes the .ini to disk.

ObjectPtr<EditorCameraProcessor> Cam = NewObject<EditorCameraProcessor>(Owner); // config applied here
Cam->ScrollSpeed = 12.0f;
Cam->SaveConfig();                                                              // persisted here
Because load runs inside NewObject, anything created the proper way picks up config for free. Objects created with raw new bypass it (and are not handle-tracked) — always spawn through NewObject<T>.
Delta-only saving
SaveConfig only writes a field whose value differs from the type’s default. The default is the value the constructor produces (captured once in the type’s DefaultInstance). Consequences:

A pristine object writes nothing — exactly like Unreal, no .ini appears until a setting actually changes.
A field reset back to its default is removed from the file on the next save.
Comparison is done on the serialized form, so it works for any field type without bespoke equality logic.

Inherited fields
Config-bound fields declared in a base class are honoured on derived instances. LoadConfig/SaveConfig walk the base chain, and inherited fields use the derived type’s section. This is how every CatalystProcessor subclass inherits the processor config fields (Priority, ExecutionGroup, …) declared on the base.
The .ini format
Standard ini — [Section] headers and Key=Value lines. On top of plain Set, array-valued keys support UE-style operators as a one-character key prefix:



































Written lineOperationEffectKey=ValueSetReplace the key’s value+Key=ValueAddAppend (duplicates allowed).Key=ValueAddUniqueAppend only if not already present-Key=ValueRemoveRemove a matching entry!Key=ClearEmpty the array
These let an override layer amend a base list rather than replace it wholesale.
The cascade (override layers)
Reads for a Local file resolve through a layered cascade, later layers overriding earlier ones:
Engine defaults  ->  Project (Saved/Config)  ->  User (Documents override)

This means a project can ship sensible defaults, a machine can override them locally, and a user override can sit on top — all without editing the shipped file. Global files are single-layer (just the one source-controlled file). Writes always target the writable layer (Project for Global; Project/Saved for Local), and array operators are resolved across the cascade so amendments compose.
Imperative access: ConfigStore
For settings that are not bound to an object field — or when you want to read/write config directly — use ConfigStore, the engine’s GConfig equivalent. It resolves a (scope, file) to a path, caches the parsed file, and exposes typed access:
ConfigStore& Store = ConfigStore::Get();
 
bool Vsync = true;
Store.GetBool(ConfigScope::Global, "Renderer", "/Render", "VSync", Vsync);
 
Store.SetInt(ConfigScope::Local, "Editor", "/Window", "Width", 1920);
Store.Flush(ConfigScope::Local, "Editor");                 // write to disk

Scalars: GetBool/Int/Float/String and matching setters.
Arrays: GetArray, AddArrayItem (with Unique), RemoveArrayItem, ClearArray — these emit the +/./-/! operators across the cascade.
Any reflected/codec type: the generic Get<T> / Set<T>, which route through ConfigArchive (below).
Flush writes the cached file; Reload drops the cache so the next read re-reads disk.

The value codec: ConfigArchive
ConfigArchive is the bidirectional text codec that turns a value into its .ini string and back. It is a sibling of the binary Archive, and it decides how to encode a type in two tiers:


Tier 1 — automatic. Arithmetic types, bool, enums (by name), String, Array<T>, and any reflected struct (recursed field-by-field) are handled with no extra code. Just adding [[LocalConfig]] to such a field is enough.


Tier 2 — custom escape hatch. A type can define its own text form by providing two members:
String ToConfigString() const;
void   FromConfigString(StringView Text);
The codec detects these (duck-typed) and uses them instead of recursing. This is for types whose ini representation should be compact and hand-editable rather than a struct dump.


Built-in Tier-2 codecs ship for:

Tag — persisted by its stable full name (e.g. Gameplay.Damage.Fire), not its runtime index.
SoftAssetPtr<T> — persisted as its asset path.
TagQuery — persisted in functional form, e.g. All(Tag(A), Not(Tag(B))).

Worked example
// Declaration
struct [[Reflect]] GraphicsSettings : public ::Object
{
    [[LocalConfig("Graphics")]] int32  ShadowResolution = 2048;
    [[LocalConfig("Graphics")]] bool   Bloom            = true;
    [[LocalConfig("Graphics")]] String Preset           = String("High");
};
 
// Usage
ObjectPtr<GraphicsSettings> Settings = NewObject<GraphicsSettings>(Owner); // loads any saved values
Settings->ShadowResolution = 4096;
Settings->SaveConfig();                                                    // writes only the changed field
Resulting ProjectDir/Saved/Config/Graphics.ini (note: Bloom and Preset were left at their defaults, so they are not written):
[/Class/Game/GraphicsSettings]
ShadowResolution=4096
See Also

Property Attribute — the general [[...]] attribute mechanism these tags belong to
Objects — NewObject<T> and where LoadConfig runs in the object lifecycle
Processor — processors are config-bound through their CatalystProcessor base
Tags / Tag Query — types with built-in Tier-2 config codecs

Property-Attributes/Property-Attributes.title: Property Attributes
Property-Attributes/Property-Attributes.content:
Property attributes are C++ [[attribute]] tags you attach to reflected fields, structs and classes. The reflector (Tools/Reflector.py) reads them at build time and bakes them into the type’s reflection data, where any system can query them at runtime. They are the engine’s declarative way to describe a property — “this field is a config setting”, “show this under the Lens category”, “never serialize this” — without writing any imperative registration code.
struct [[Reflect]] CameraSettings
{
    [[Category("Lens"), Tooltip("Vertical field of view, in degrees")]]
    float FieldOfView = 60.0f;
 
    [[LocalConfig("EditorCamera")]]
    float ScrollSpeed = 5.0f;
 
    [[Transient]]
    float CachedAspectRatio = 0.0f;
};
How it works

You annotate a declaration with one or more [[Name]] or [[Name("argument")]] attributes. Multiple are comma-separated inside a single [[ ]].
The reflector parses them while generating the type’s .Generated.cpp, storing each attribute (and its optional string argument) on the corresponding FieldInfo (for fields) or TypeInfo (for the struct/class).
At runtime, any system queries them through reflection:

const FieldInfo& Field = /* ... */;
if (Field.HasMetadata("LocalConfig"))
{
    String File = Field.GetMetadata("LocalConfig");   // the string argument, e.g. "EditorCamera"
}

The runtime reflection API still spells these HasMetadata / GetMetadata — “metadata” is the internal storage term. Conceptually they are the property attributes described here.

An attribute is therefore passive — adding [[LocalConfig("...")]] does nothing on its own. It is the consuming system (here, the config system) that walks a type’s fields, looks for its attributes, and acts on them. This keeps the declaration site clean and the behaviour centralised.
Field-level vs type-level

Type-level attributes sit on the struct/class itself: struct [[Reflect, Abstract]] CatalystProcessor.
Field-level attributes sit on a member: [[Category("Lens")]] float FieldOfView;.

Some are meaningful in both positions (e.g. DisplayName); most are specific to one.
Property attributes vs Annotations
Property attributes are not the same as the Annotations system, and they solve different problems:



































Property Attribute ([[...]])Annotations (ADD_ANNOTATION)WhenCompile-time attribute, parsed by the reflectorRuntime, registered via REGISTER_ANNOTATIONSValueA string (or just a flag)An arbitrary C++ object (EditorIcon, ConstructorAnnotation, …)GranularityPer field and per typePer typeMutableNo — fixed at build timeYes — a plugin can add/override at runtimeUse forSimple declarative tags on a propertyRich per-type behaviour and editor data
Rule of thumb: reach for a property attribute to tag an individual property with intent; reach for an annotation when a type needs to carry a behaviour or a non-trivial object.
Common attributes
This catalogue is not exhaustive — systems and modules define their own attributes, and the authoritative list is whatever the reflector recognises plus each system’s own documentation. Frequently used ones include:


















































AttributeTargetMeaningReflecttypeMarks a struct/class for reflection (required for everything else).AbstracttypeThe type is a non-instantiable base.TransientfieldExclude the field from serialization.Category("...")fieldEditor grouping the field appears under.Tooltip("...")fieldEditor hover description.DisplayName("...")type / fieldHuman-readable name shown in the editor.LocalConfig("File")fieldPersist the field to a per-machine config file. See Config Attribute.GlobalConfig("File")fieldPersist the field to a team-shared, source-controlled config file. See Config Attribute.
See Also

Config Attribute — [[LocalConfig]] / [[GlobalConfig]] and the config system they drive
Annotations — the runtime, per-type metadata registry
Objects — Object, NewObject<T>, and where config is applied
Components — the heaviest user of reflected fields and their attributes

RHI/RHI.title: RHI
RHI/RHI.content:
RHI (Rendering Hardware Interface)
The Rendering Hardware Interface (RHI) is Alchemy Engine’s abstraction layer that decouples the core engine logic from specific graphics APIs (like Vulkan). This ensures that the engine remains hardware-agnostic and future-proof.
Architecture
The RHI architecture consists of three main components:

IRenderInterface: The abstract base class defining the contract for all rendering backends.
RenderSubsystem: The high-level engine subsystem that manages the active RHI and provides a user-friendly API to the rest of the engine.
VulkanRHI: The concrete implementation of IRenderInterface using the Vulkan API.

IRenderInterface
The IRenderInterface is the seam every backend implements. Crucially, it speaks only backend-neutral types — the value types and opaque handles in Renderer/RHITypes.h (Extent2D, RHIFormat, RHITextureUsage, TextureHandle, RHIBufferHandle, RHIPipelineHandle, RHIRenderTargetHandle), the pipeline/vertex/push-constant descriptions in Renderer/RHIPipeline.h, the abstract RHICommandList, and the opaque IRenderSurface. No Vk* type or <vulkan/vulkan.h> appears in the interface, so any code above the RHI can call it without depending on a graphics API.
It covers:

Lifecycle: Initialize(), Shutdown(), WaitIdle().
Frame / present: BeginFrame(), SetActiveSurface(), BeginSwapchainRenderPass(), EndFrame(), IsFrameInProgress(), GetCurrentFrameIndex(), GetFramesInFlight(), GetSurfaceExtent().
Surfaces: CreateSurface(nativeWindow) / DestroySurface() — one IRenderSurface per OS window. The window layer holds surfaces as opaque handles and never names the concrete swapchain type.
Textures: handle-based CreateTexture2D(), UploadTextureRegion(), DestroyTexture(), GetOrCreateTexture(), plus the bindless queries IsBindlessSupported() / GetBindlessIndex().
Buffers & pipelines: CreateBuffer() / DestroyBuffer() / GetBufferMappedPtr() (host-visible mapped buffers), and CreateGraphicsPipeline(desc) / DestroyPipeline() built from a neutral RHIPipelineDesc (shader bytecode, vertex layout, push constants, uniform bindings, blend/depth/cull state).
Render targets: CreateRenderTarget() / ResizeRenderTarget() / DestroyRenderTarget() for offscreen color+depth targets, plus GetRenderTargetImGuiTextureID() to sample one as an ImGui image (how the editor’s world viewport is drawn).
Command recording: GetCommandList() returns an abstract RHICommandList — BindPipeline, BindVertexBuffer / BindIndexBuffer, PushConstants, BindUniformBuffer, BindBindlessTextures, DrawIndexed, and BeginRenderTarget / EndRenderTarget.
Dear ImGui: InitImGuiForWindow(), NewImGuiFrame(), RenderImGui(), ShutdownImGui() — the backend owns the API-specific ImGui shim so UI code stays agnostic.

RenderSubsystem
The RenderSubsystem is an IEngineSubsystem that owns the lifecycle of the RHI. It handles the high-level orchestration of the rendering loop and ensures that other engine systems (like the ImGuiSubsystem) can interact with the GPU safely.
Key responsibilities:

Managing the transition between frame states (BeginFrame / EndFrame).
Providing access to the underlying RHI for specialized systems.
Handling window-system dependencies.

Vulkan Implementation
The current primary backend is VulkanRHI. It leverages modern practices to ensure high performance and stability:

No Vulkan SDK required: Vulkan is de-SDK’d. The engine vendors Vulkan-Headers and the volk meta-loader (under Source/ThirdParty), and volk loads the system vulkan-1.dll — installed by the GPU driver on every machine — at runtime via volkInitialize / volkLoadInstance / volkLoadDevice. There is no import library and nothing to install to build or run. Because the whole build compiles with VK_NO_PROTOTYPES, every vk* call resolves through a volk function pointer.
Vulkan Memory Allocator (VMA): Used for efficient GPU memory management and allocation. Configured to fetch its entry points dynamically from volk (no statically-linked prototypes).
GLM: Used for all internal vector and matrix mathematics (column-major, which the shader compiler is set to match).
Validation Layers: Enabled in Debug builds when the validation layer is present. Without it (e.g. no SDK installed) the engine detects the absence and runs unvalidated rather than failing instance creation.
Swapchain Management: Handles automatic window resizing and surface acquisition.

Future Backends
Adding a new backend (e.g. a hypothetical DirectX12RHI) means implementing IRenderInterface and changing one line in the factory — CreateRHI() in Renderer/RHIFactory.cpp is the single place the engine names a concrete backend. Subsystems, windowing, the editor, the asset caches, and the GPU-native renderers (SceneRenderer, SigilRenderer) all hold only an IRenderInterface and record through the neutral RHICommandList, so they are unaffected.
The one remaining backend-shaped value is shader bytecode. RHIShaderBytecode carries SPIR-V. Shaders are authored in Slang and compiled to SPIR-V by the ShaderLibrary (see Shaders). A second backend would ask the same Slang compiler for a different target (DXIL, MSL, …) rather than introduce a new shading language — the authoring surface stays the same.
Integration with ImGui
The ImGuiSubsystem is fully decoupled from the graphics API: it drives the editor UI through IRenderInterface’s ImGui methods (InitImGuiForWindow, NewImGuiFrame, RenderImGui, ShutdownImGui). The API-specific ImGui platform+renderer backends (imgui_impl_vulkan / imgui_impl_glfw) live entirely inside VulkanRHI, so the subsystem never includes a backend header or names a Vk* type.
Usage Example
From the engine core or an application, rendering is controlled via the RenderSubsystem:
auto* RenderSys = Engine::Get()->GetSubsystem<RenderSubsystem>();
 
RenderSys->BeginFrame();
// ... logic to record draw commands ...
RenderSys->RenderUI(ImGui::GetDrawData());
RenderSys->EndFrame();
See Also

Shaders — authoring shaders in Slang, and how the ShaderLibrary compiles them
Subsystems — RenderSubsystem is an IEngineSubsystem

String-Types/String-Types.title: String Types
String-Types/String-Types.content:
String Types
Alchemy Engine provides its own owning and non-owning string types rather than using std::string directly. They are tuned for the access patterns a game engine actually has: lots of short strings (asset paths, identifiers, tags), keys compared repeatedly, and a strong preference for avoiding allocations.
The engine migrated off std::string to these types — prefer them throughout engine and game code, in line with the Style guide’s “types over std” guidance.
The Types




















TypeOwns data?Use forStringYesThe default owning string. Small-string optimization, a cached hash for fast repeated comparison, and first-class UTF-8 byte/character bookkeeping.StringViewNoA read-only, non-owning window over existing string data. Use for function parameters and lookups where you don’t need to take ownership — avoids copies entirely.
See String for the full reference: small-string optimization, the cached hash, the bytes-vs-characters distinction, UTF-8 handling, memory layout, performance characteristics, and gotchas.
See Also

String — the detailed String / String View reference
Style guide — the convention to prefer Alchemy::Types over std

String-Types/String.title: String
String-Types/String.content:
String
String is Alchemy Engine’s owning string type — the engine’s equivalent of std::string, but built around the access patterns a game engine actually has: lots of short strings (asset paths, identifiers, tags), keys compared over and over, and a strong preference for avoiding allocations.
It is not a thin wrapper. String makes a few deliberate trades — it is slightly larger than std::string — in exchange for small-string optimization, a cached hash for fast repeated comparison, and first-class UTF-8 byte/character bookkeeping.


                  
                  If you only need to look at a string
                  
                

Most “read-only” work (slicing, searching, comparing a substring) does not need an owning String at all. Reach for String View, which is a non-owning window and avoids allocation entirely.


Why it exists
String targets three things std::string does not give you out of the box:

Small-string optimization (SSO) that keeps short strings entirely inline — no heap allocation.
A cached hash, so a string used as a key and compared repeatedly costs O(1) per comparison instead of a full byte scan.
Explicit UTF-8 semantics — it always knows both its byte length and its character (code-point) count, and never conflates them.

Small-string optimization
A String stores up to 23 bytes inline (24 including the null terminator). Below that threshold there is no heap allocation at all — construction is just a copy into the object’s own storage. Once a string exceeds the inline capacity it transparently moves to the heap and can grow to any size.
This is why short strings are effectively free to create. In practice the overwhelming majority of engine strings — component names, short identifiers, tags, file stems — never touch the heap.
The practical guidance: keep strings short where you can, and they cost you almost nothing.
Bytes vs. characters
This is the most important thing to understand about String, and the easiest place to introduce a bug if you ignore it.
UTF-8 encodes a character (code point) in one to four bytes. So "Hello, 世界" is 9 characters but 13 bytes (the two CJK characters are 3 bytes each). String tracks both, and exposes them as two clearly separated queries:























You want…UseReturnsUse it forThe raw storage sizeGetByteSize()bytesbuffers, indexing, memory, interop with C APIsThe user-visible lengthGetLength()code pointsUI, cursor positions, “how many characters”
Everything internal works in bytes. Indexing (operator[]), Find, and Substring all use byte offsets, not character offsets — consistent with how the data is actually laid out. GetByteSize() is O(1) (it is stored); GetLength() is computed on demand by walking the bytes, since the character count is rarely needed in hot paths.


                  
                  Don't assume one byte per character 
                  
                

A common reflex carried over from ASCII is to treat size as both “bytes” and “characters.” For any non-ASCII content these differ. If you are sizing a buffer, you want GetByteSize(). If you are counting what a human sees, you want GetLength().


UTF-8 source literals
String is built to hold UTF-8 bytes. A u8"..." literal is always UTF-8 regardless of compiler settings and is the safest way to embed non-ASCII text. Plain narrow "..." literals also work because the engine compiles with MSVC’s /utf-8 flag (which forces both the source and execution character sets to UTF-8) — without that flag, narrow literals would be interpreted in the machine’s local code page and non-ASCII text would be mangled.
The cached hash
String caches a 64-bit FNV-1a hash of its contents. The cache is lazy: the hash is only computed the first time it is needed (an explicit GetHash(), or a case-sensitive comparison), and a value of 0 is used as the “dirty” sentinel — any mutation resets the cache to 0, so it is recomputed on next use.
The payoff is fast repeated comparison. Case-sensitive equality first compares byte length, then the cached hashes; only if the hashes match does it confirm with an actual byte comparison (to rule out the vanishingly rare 64-bit collision). For two strings compared many times — exactly what happens with map keys, asset IDs, and lookups — every comparison after the first is effectively O(1).


                  
                  When the cached hash helps, and when it doesn't 
                  
                


Helps: long-ish strings compared repeatedly (map keys, IDs). The first compare warms the hash; the rest are O(1).
Doesn’t help: very short strings (a few-byte memcmp is already faster than the hash machinery), or a string compared exactly once (you pay a full hash pass and then a compare — slower than just comparing).

The design assumes you “modify rarely, compare often.” If you plan to compare a string a lot, mutate it as little as possible so the cached hash stays warm.


Memory layout
String is 40 bytes, with zero padding. The fields are packed deliberately:
CachedHash   8 bytes   (0 means "dirty / needs recompute")
Capacity     4 bytes   (top bit = "data is on the heap"; low 31 bits = real capacity)
ByteSize     4 bytes   (storage size in bytes, excluding the null terminator)
union        24 bytes  (inline char buffer, OR a heap pointer)

Two pieces of state that naive string types spend whole fields on are folded in: the “hash is dirty” flag is encoded as CachedHash == 0, and the “using heap” flag is the top bit of Capacity (an SSO string never needs a 2-gigabyte capacity, so the bit is free). The heap flag is stored, not inferred from the capacity’s magnitude — so a future change to growth or capacity handling can’t silently misclassify inline vs. heap storage.
For comparison, MSVC’s std::string is 32 bytes. String trades those extra 8 bytes for SSO plus the cached hash. If you are storing hundreds of thousands of strings and memory is critical, prefer String View for the read-only ones.
String View
StringView is a non-owning window into character data it does not control. It stores only a pointer and a byte length, so constructing one and slicing it is effectively free — no allocation, no copy.
Use it whenever you need to read or slice without taking ownership:
String Path = "Assets/Textures/Brick.alchemyasset";
 
// Owning substring: allocates and copies.
String Owned = Path.Substring(7);
 
// View substring: a pointer + length. Zero allocation.
StringView Slice = Path.View().Substring(7);
Both forms support the same read-only operations — Find, StartsWith, EndsWith, Contains, Equals, byte/character counts.


                  
                  A view does not own its data 
                  
                

A StringView is only valid while the data it points at stays alive and unchanged. A view obtained from a String is invalidated the moment that String is mutated (a mutation may reallocate) or destroyed. Treat views as short-lived; never store one past the lifetime of its source.


A view is also not guaranteed to be null-terminated — Substring can carve a slice out of the middle of a buffer. Every StringView operation is therefore length-bounded and never relies on a terminator. (String, by contrast, is always null-terminated, so its GetCharData() is safe to hand to C APIs.)
Performance characteristics
Representative numbers from a desktop run. Absolute timings are machine-dependent — what matters is the relative comparison within each pairing. Check the host’s CPU/RAM before drawing conclusions about a specific number.













































Scenarions/opTakeawaySlice → owning String (alloc + copy)~114Owning a slice costs an allocation…Slice → StringView (zero alloc)~7…so view it instead when you only read it. (~16× cheaper)Construct short (≤23 B, inline/SSO)~14Short strings never touch the heap…Construct long (>23 B, heap)~115…the ~100 ns delta is the malloc/free round-trip.Append ×20000 (geometric)~17Incremental building is amortized O(n) — += in a loop is fine.String == (cached hash, warm)~5Repeated equality on a long key is O(1)…std::string == (memcmp ~512 B)~17…vs. a full byte scan every time.
The headline reads: view, don’t copy, when you only read; keep strings short to stay off the heap; lean on equality for keys you compare often.


                  
                  Appending in a loop 
                  
                

Repeated s += x / Append grows capacity geometrically, so building a string piece by piece is amortized O(n). Reserve exists if you know the final size up front and want to skip the intermediate growth.


Gotchas

Byte indexing. operator[], Find, and Substring use byte offsets. Indexing into the middle of a multibyte code point gives you a single byte, not a character.
Non-const access invalidates the cached hash. A non-const operator[] or begin() hands out writable access, so it conservatively marks the hash dirty (it cannot know whether you will write). Prefer const access in hot read paths so the cached hash stays warm.
View lifetime. As above — a StringView does not keep its source alive and is invalidated by any mutation of the source.
Case conversion is ASCII-only. ToLower/ToUpper fold ASCII letters and leave multibyte code points untouched. Full Unicode case folding is out of scope.
Ordering is byte-lexicographic. Comparison operators sort by raw bytes, which for UTF-8 also matches code-point order — suitable for ordered containers and sorting.

See Also

String Types — overview of the engine’s string family
Profiler — Tracy profiling; String zones fire on actual heap allocations
Testing Guide — the benchmark harness these numbers come from

Subsystems.title: Subsystems
Subsystems.content:
Subsystems
A subsystem is a singleton process with a controlled lifetime.

A world subsystem is created and destroyed as a world is created and destroyed.
A engine subsystem is created and destroyed as the engine is started and closed.

Auto registration
For a subsystem to be discovered by the reflection system, you must mark it with the [[Reflect]] attribute.
Subsystems can opt out of auto registration, mark it with the [[Abstract]] attribute.
Dependencies
Subsystems are able to control their initialization and tick order by adding a dependency onto another subsystem through the AddDependency function.
You can also use Priority to control the order of subsystems processing.
For example; the ImGui subsystem has a dependency on the WindowSubsystem, as ImGui requires a window to be valid to function.
See Also

Resonance — the messaging system subsystems use to communicate

Type-Registry.title: Type Registry
Type-Registry.content:
Type Registry
The Type Registry is the central reflection database for the Alchemy Engine. It maintains runtime type information for every [[Reflect]]-marked struct and enum in the engine, enabling systems like Catalyst, serialization, config binding, and the editor to query type metadata without compile-time dependencies.
The registry lives in Source/Engine/Reflection/TypeRegistry.h and is part of the Alchemy namespace.
Overview
When a struct or enum is marked with the [[Reflect]] attribute, the Reflector (a build-time code generator) emits registration code that inserts a TypeInfo entry into the global TypeRegistry. Each entry carries:

Identity — name, owning module, and a unique numeric ID
Layout — size, field offsets, and per-field type names
Lifecycle — constructor, destructor, copy and move function pointers
Serialization — binary and config-text codec function pointers
Catalyst metadata — element indices, sparse storage flag, zero-size flag
Default instance — a byte snapshot of a default-constructed value

Systems query the registry by type (template), by name (string), by ID (uint32), or by std::type_index to resolve this metadata at runtime.
TypeInfo
Every registered type gets a TypeInfo struct. This is the primary data structure the registry stores and returns.
Identity Fields

























FieldTypeDescriptionNameStringBare type name (e.g. "TransformComponent")ModuleStringOwning module name (e.g. "Engine", "Editor_AlchemyEditor"). Empty for hand-registered typesIDuint32_tUnique numeric identifier assigned at registration time, incrementing from 0
The fully qualified name is computed via GetQualifiedName(), which returns "<Module>.<Name>" (e.g. "Engine.TransformComponent"). This qualified form is the primary key used in the internal storage map and in serialization.
Layout Fields

























FieldTypeDescriptionSizesize_tsizeof(T) for the registered typeFieldsArray<FieldInfo>Ordered list of reflected fields with name, type name, offset, and sizeBaseTypeNameStringBare name of the primary base class. Supplied by the Reflector for inheritance-aware systems like config binding
FieldInfo
Each reflected field is described by a FieldInfo struct:

Registration
Types are registered at DLL load time through generated code emitted by the Reflector. There are four registration paths:
RegisterStruct<T>(Name, Module)
Registers a reflected struct. The Reflector generates a call like:
TypeRegistry::RegisterStruct<MyComponent>("MyComponent", "Engine");
During registration the system:

Creates a module-qualified key ("Engine.MyComponent")
Allocates a unique ID
Sets Size, IsZeroSizeElement, and StorageIsSparse via compile-time checks
Assigns lifecycle function pointers based on SFINAE trait checks
Snapshots a default-constructed instance into DefaultInstance
Maps std::type_index(typeid(T)) to the TypeInfo pointer

The TypeToInfo mapping is updated every time RegisterStruct is called (even for duplicates), which ensures the pointer stays valid across DLL reloads.
RegisterEnum<T>(Name, Module)
Registers a reflected enum. Similar to RegisterStruct but sets IsEnum = true and provides trivial lifecycle functions. Generated code then chains AddEnumerator() calls to populate the value-to-name table:
TypeRegistry::RegisterEnum<MyEnum>("MyEnum", "Engine")
    .AddEnumerator(0, "First")
    .AddEnumerator(5, "Second");
RegisterSlotType(BaseType, SlotName)
Creates a virtual TypeInfo that clones an existing type’s layout and lifecycle but receives a unique ID and name. Used by the Catalyst slot system to give each (ComponentType, SlotID) pair its own archetype column.
RegisterVirtualType(Name, IsSparse)
Fabricates a standalone, zero-size TypeInfo with no fields or lifecycle functions. Used by Catalyst’s virtual (string-named) markers — the name is the fully qualified registry name (e.g. "$Marker$Player") and IsSparse fixes the storage policy for the type’s lifetime.
Lookup
The registry provides five lookup overloads:
GetType<T>()
Template-based lookup using std::type_index(typeid(T)). Fastest path — no string comparison. Returns nullptr if the type is not registered.
TypeInfo* Info = TypeRegistry::GetType<TransformComponent>();
GetType(string_view Name)
String-based lookup with two-phase resolution:

Exact key match — attempts a direct lookup in the Types map. Hits when passed a module-qualified name (e.g. "Engine.TransformComponent") or a bare name for hand-registered types
Bare name fallback — if the exact key misses, strips any <Module>. prefix and searches the BareNameIndex. If multiple modules share the same bare name and no module filter is provided, a warning is logged and the first candidate is returned

TypeInfo* Info = TypeRegistry::GetType("TransformComponent");
TypeInfo* Info = TypeRegistry::GetType("Engine.TransformComponent");
GetType(uint32_t ID)
ID-based lookup via the IDToInfo array. O(1) access.
GetType(type_index Index)
Runtime type-index lookup. Used by Object to resolve its dynamic type via typeid(*this).
GetAllTypes()
Returns a const reference to the complete Types map. Used by the runtime module to iterate all registered types.
Instance Management
The Type Registry supports a dual-instance model to handle DLL boundaries:
Local Instance
Each DLL has its own local registry (a function-local static returned by GetLocalInstance()). During DLL load, static initializers and generated registration code populate this local instance.
Active Instance
A single static pointer (ActiveInstance) designates the “authoritative” registry. By default, Get() returns the local instance. Once the engine calls SetInstance(), all subsequent Get() calls return the active instance.
Merge Flow
When a DLL loads, the typical sequence is:

Static initializers register types into the DLL’s local TypeRegistry
OnLoad is called on the DLL module
The module calls Merge() to copy all types from the local registry into the host registry
SetInstance() redirects subsequent lookups to the host

// Inside a DLL's OnLoad:
TypeRegistry* HostRegistry = static_cast<TypeRegistry*>(Context->TypeRegistryInstance);
HostRegistry->Merge(TypeRegistry::GetLocalInstance());
TypeRegistry::SetInstance(HostRegistry);
This ensures all modules share a single type database after initialization, while each DLL can register its own types independently during load.
Plugin Context
The EditorContext struct (passed from host to guest DLLs) carries a TypeRegistryInstance field as void*. Plugins cast this back to TypeRegistry* to access the shared registry.

Usage in Engine Systems
The Type Registry is queried extensively throughout the engine:

Catalyst — Every component add/remove/query operation resolves TypeInfo* to determine element indices, storage policy, and archetype columns. Function-local static TypeInfo* caches are used to pay the lookup cost exactly once per type per binary lifetime
Object System — Object::LoadConfig and SaveConfig walk the inheritance chain by resolving BaseTypeName through the registry
Config Binding — ConfigArchive uses ConfigSerialize function pointers and per-field ConfigCodec thunks to serialize types to/from config text
Entity Library Serialization — Resolves component types by name when loading saved entity libraries
Editor Details Panel — Uses DefaultInstance byte snapshots to detect modified fields and support per-field reset
Instanced Struct — Validates that the requested type matches the stored TypeInfo before casting
Tag System — Resolves tag container types through the registry

Best Practices

Always use GetType<T>() (template) when the type is known at compile time — it avoids string lookups entirely
Cache TypeInfo* results in function-local static variables when calling repeatedly in hot paths
Use module-qualified names in GetType(string) when possible to avoid ambiguity warnings
Never call registration methods from multiple threads — registration happens at DLL load time on a single thread
The registry is not thread-safe during Merge() — ensure merges complete before spawning worker threads
